LCAT esterifies cholesterol within HDL, forming cholesteryl esters (CE) that constitute the core of mature HDL particles.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · HDL METABOLISM AND REVERSE CHOLESTEROL TRANSPORT

items.subtopic: HDL METABOLISM AND REVERSE CHOLESTEROL TRANSPORT
items.title: Pathways for Hepatic Uptake and Excretion of HDL Cholesterol
items.content: HDL cholesterol is transported to hepatocytes via two major pathways: selective uptake by SR-B1, which allows CE transfer without particle dissociation, and CETP-mediated exchange with apoB-containing lipoproteins. Once in the liver, HDL-derived CE can be hydrolyzed and excreted into bile or converted to bile acids for biliary excretion. Additionally, transintestinal cholesterol excretion provides a direct route from HDL to the intestinal lumen.
keyPoints: HDL CE is taken up by hepatocytes via SR-B1-mediated selective uptake.
CETP mediates exchange of HDL CE with apoB-containing lipoproteins.
Hepatocyte hydrolysis and bile excretion are key steps in cholesterol elimination.
Transintestinal cholesterol excretion allows direct transport from HDL to the intestinal lumen.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · HDL METABOLISM AND REVERSE CHOLESTEROL TRANSPORT

items.title: Importance of Screening for Dyslipidemia in ASCVD Risk Reduction
items.content: Screening for dyslipidemia is crucial as it plays a significant role in the development of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD). Active screening with a lipid panel, preferably after an overnight fast, helps identify individuals at risk and allows for interventions that can substantially reduce cardiovascular risk.
keyPoints: Dyslipidemia is a major causal factor in ASCVD.
Screening should be conducted on all adults and many children.
Lipid panel includes total cholesterol, triglycerides (TGs), HDL-C, and estimated LDL-C using the Friedewald formula: LDL-C = total cholesterol - (TG/5) - HDL-C.
The Friedewald formula is accurate if TG levels are <200 mg/dL; not used if TG >400 mg/dL.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · SCREENING

items.title: Advanced Lipid Markers for Cardiovascular Risk Assessment
items.content: Beyond traditional lipid panels, measuring plasma apoB and Lp(a) levels can provide a more comprehensive assessment of cardiovascular risk. ApoB is increasingly recognized as a better marker than LDL-C or non-HDL-C due to its strong association with ASCVD risk.
keyPoints: Measurement of plasma apoB may offer superior cardiovascular risk assessment compared to LDL-C and non-HDL-C levels.
Lp(a) is an independent ASCVD risk factor, highly heritable, useful for risk stratification.
While not standard practice yet, evidence supports the use of apoB as a guide for therapeutic intervention.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · SCREENING

items.subtopic: DISORDERS ASSOCIATED WITH ELEVATED APOB-CONTAINING LIPOPROTEINS
items.title: Why Identify Elevated ApoB-Containing Lipoproteins?
items.content: Identifying disorders associated with elevated apoB-containing lipoproteins is crucial due to their strong association with increased risk for atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD) and acute pancreatitis. Early diagnosis allows for interventions that can significantly reduce these risks.
keyPoints: Elevated levels of total cholesterol, TGs, LDL-C, or both are characteristic.
Hyperlipidemia often involves genetic predisposition and environmental factors.
Intervention in hyperlipidemia can substantially reduce ASCVD risk.
Severe hypertriglyceridemia may increase the risk for acute pancreatitis.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · DISORDERS ASSOCIATED WITH ELEVATED APOB-CONTAINING LIPOPROTEINS

items.subtopic: DISORDERS ASSOCIATED WITH ELEVATED APOB-CONTAINING LIPOPROTEINS
items.title: How Do Genetic Disorders Inform Lipoprotein Metabolism?
items.content: Primary genetic disorders of lipoprotein metabolism, caused by single-gene mutations, provide insights into the physiological roles of specific proteins in key pathways regulating lipoprotein metabolism. Understanding these can guide diagnosis and treatment.
keyPoints: Key pathways include lipolysis of TG-rich lipoproteins, receptor-mediated uptake of apoB-containing lipoproteins, cellular cholesterol metabolism, VLDL assembly/secretion, and neutral lipid transfer/phospholipid hydrolysis.
Single-gene mutations in these pathways offer insights into protein functions and dyslipidemia mechanisms.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · DISORDERS ASSOCIATED WITH ELEVATED APOB-CONTAINING LIPOPROTEINS

items.title: Diagnosis and Clinical Presentation of Familial Chylomicronemia Syndrome
items.content: Familial chylomicronemia syndrome (FCS) is a genetic disorder characterized by severe hypertriglyceridemia due to LPL deficiency or dysfunction, leading to impaired lipolysis of triglycerides. Clinically, FCS presents with fasting TG levels >500 mg/dL and can cause acute pancreatitis and eruptive xanthomas.
keyPoints: Fasting TG levels >500 mg/dL are diagnostic for severe hypertriglyceridemia in the context of FCS.
Clinical features include acute pancreatitis, eruptive xanthomas, lipemia retinalis, and sometimes hepatosplenomegaly.
FCS is caused by mutations affecting LPL activity or processing (e.g., LPL gene, APOC2, APOA5, GPIHBP1, LMF1).
The condition can present in both childhood and adulthood.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · SEVERE HYPERTRIGLYCERIDEMIA (part 1)

items.title: Genetic Causes of Familial Chylomicronemia Syndrome
items.content: FCS results from genetic mutations affecting lipoprotein lipase (LPL) activity or processing. These include LPL gene mutations, APOC2 and APOA5 deficiencies, GPIHBP1 mutations, and LMF1 mutations, all leading to impaired triglyceride hydrolysis.
keyPoints: Mutations in the LPL gene are the most common cause of FCS.
APOC2 and APOA5 deficiencies result from loss-of-function mutations affecting LPL cofactors.
GPIHBP1 mutations disrupt LPL transport to endothelial surfaces, causing FCS.
LMF1 is essential for LPL processing; its mutations can lead to FCS.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · SEVERE HYPERTRIGLYCERIDEMIA (part 1)

items.title: Clinical Management and Diagnostic Approach for Severe Hypertriglyceridemia
items.content: Severe hypertriglyceridemia, particularly when associated with acute pancreatitis or eruptive xanthomas, should prompt consideration of FCS. Diagnosis is clinical but can be confirmed through genetic testing of candidate genes.
keyPoints: Fasting TG levels >500 mg/dL in the context of acute pancreatitis suggest severe hypertriglyceridemia.
Eruptive xanthomas and lipemia retinalis are indicative of FCS.
Genetic testing can confirm mutations in LPL, APOC2, APOA5, GPIHBP1, or LMF1.
LPL activity assays are not widely available but can be used for diagnosis.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · SEVERE HYPERTRIGLYCERIDEMIA (part 1)

items.title: Therapeutic Goals in Familial Chylomicronemia Syndrome
items.content: The primary goal in managing familial chylomicronemia syndrome (FCS) is to prevent pancreatitis by reducing fasting triglyceride (TG) levels below 500 mg/dL. This involves strict dietary fat restriction, often supplemented with fat-soluble vitamins, and may include the use of fish oils or fibrates like fenofibrate, although their effectiveness is limited.
keyPoints: Prevent pancreatitis by reducing fasting TG levels to <500 mg/dL
Dietary fat intake should be restricted to as little as 15 g/day
Consultation with a registered dietician is essential
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · SEVERE HYPERTRIGLYCERIDEMIA (part 2)

items.title: Management Challenges in Familial Partial Lipodystrophy
items.content: Familial partial lipodystrophy (FPLD) is characterized by impaired adipose tissue generation, leading to severe hypertriglyceridemia (HTG), insulin resistance, and increased atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD) risk. Management includes aggressive treatment with statins and LDL-lowering therapies, alongside managing diabetes and potential progression of fatty liver disease.
keyPoints: FPLD is an underrecognized cause of severe HTG
Characterized by loss of subcutaneous fat in extremities and increased visceral fat
Management includes aggressive treatment with statins and LDL-lowering therapies
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · SEVERE HYPERTRIGLYCERIDEMIA (part 2)

items.title: Approach to Multifactorial Severe Hypertriglyceridemia
items.content: Most patients with severe hypertriglyceridemia have a multifactorial etiology, combining genetic and environmental factors. The prevalence of this phenotype is approximately 1 in 1000, and it often runs in families, though not typically due to single-gene mutations.
keyPoints: Multifactorial etiology includes genetics and environment
Prevalence is ~1 in 1000
HTG often runs in families
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · SEVERE HYPERTRIGLYCERIDEMIA (part 2)

items.title: Management of Severe Hypertriglyceridemia to Prevent Acute Pancreatitis
items.content: Severe hypertriglyceridemia, characterized by fasting triglyceride levels >500 mg/dL, poses a risk for acute pancreatitis. Management focuses on reducing TG levels through dietary and lifestyle changes initially. If these measures fail, drug therapy with fibrates or fish oil is recommended to prevent pancreatitis.
keyPoints: Fasting triglyceride levels >500 mg/dL increase the risk of acute pancreatitis.
Dietary and lifestyle modifications are first-line interventions for TG reduction.
Drug therapy (fibrates or fish oil) should be considered if TGs remain elevated after initial interventions.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · SEVERE HYPERTRIGLYCERIDEMIA (part 3)

items.title: Consideration of ASCVD Risk in Hypertriglyceridemia Management
items.content: In patients with severe hypertriglyceridemia, it is crucial to evaluate for atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD) risk. Those at high risk should receive statin therapy regardless of TG levels. For those not at high ASCVD risk, lifestyle changes may suffice without lipid-lowering drugs.
keyPoints: Evaluate patients with severe hypertriglyceridemia for ASCVD risk.
High-risk patients should be treated with statins to reduce cardiovascular risk.
Patients not at high ASCVD risk can often avoid lipid-lowering drugs with lifestyle modifications.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · SEVERE HYPERTRIGLYCERIDEMIA (part 3)

items.title: Understanding Familial Hypercholesterolemia (FH) and Its Genetic Basis
items.content: Familial hypercholesterolemia is an autosomal dominant disorder primarily caused by mutations in the LDLR gene, leading to reduced function of the LDL receptor. This results in elevated plasma levels of LDL-C due to decreased clearance from circulation. Other genes like APOB and PCSK9 can also cause FH through different mechanisms affecting LDL receptor activity.
keyPoints: FH is characterized by elevated LDL-C usually >190 mg/dL with normal TG levels.
Most common mutations are in the LDLR gene, but APOB and PCSK9 mutations can also cause FH.
Heterozygous FH prevalence may be as high as 1 in 250 individuals.
FH increases risk of premature ASCVD; untreated men have ~50% chance of myocardial infarction before age 60.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · HYPERCHOLESTEROLEMIA (ELEVATED LDL-C) (part 1)

items.title: Diagnosis and Screening for Familial Hypercholesterolemia
items.content: FH diagnosis is often based on elevated LDL-C levels detected during routine lipid screening, particularly in children aged 9 to 11. A family history of hypercholesterolemia or premature ASCVD should prompt targeted screening. Cascade screening within families can effectively identify additional individuals with FH.
keyPoints: Screening recommended for children between ages 9 and 11.
Family history prompts targeted screening.
Cascade screening is effective due to dominant inheritance pattern.
Physical signs may include corneal arcus and tendon xanthomas.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · HYPERCHOLESTEROLEMIA (ELEVATED LDL-C) (part 1)

items.title: Risk of Cardiovascular Disease in Heterozygous FH
items.content: Untreated heterozygous FH significantly increases the risk of cardiovascular disease. Men with untreated heterozygous FH have about a 50% chance of experiencing a myocardial infarction before age 60, while women also face substantially increased risks.
keyPoints: Untreated men with heterozygous FH have ~50% chance of myocardial infarction before age 60.
Women with heterozygous FH are at substantially increased risk of cardiovascular disease.
Risk varies based on molecular defect, LDL-C levels, and coexisting factors.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · HYPERCHOLESTEROLEMIA (ELEVATED LDL-C) (part 1)

items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Familial Hypercholesterolemia (FH)
items.content: Familial hypercholesterolemia is diagnosed based on clinical criteria including LDL-C levels >190 mg/dL without secondary causes, with a family history of hypercholesterolemia or premature ASCVD. Gene sequencing for FH-related genes can confirm the diagnosis and guide aggressive treatment.
keyPoints: Diagnosis: LDL-C >190 mg/dL in absence of secondary etiology
Family history of hypercholesterolemia/premature ASCVD supportive
Gene panel (LDLR, APOB, PCSK9) confirms diagnosis and guides treatment
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · HYPERCHOLESTEROLEMIA (ELEVATED LDL-C) (part 2)

items.title: Treatment Strategies for Heterozygous FH
items.content: Heterozygous FH patients require dietary changes and pharmacologic therapy, starting with high-intensity statins. Additional treatments include ezetimibe, PCSK9 inhibitors, ACL inhibitors, bile acid sequestrants, or LDL apheresis if necessary.
keyPoints: Diet low in saturated/trans fats recommended
High-intensity statin therapy as initial treatment
Additional drugs: ezetimibe, PCSK9 inhibitors, ACL inhibitors, bile acid sequestrants
Consider LDL apheresis for inadequate control
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · HYPERCHOLESTEROLEMIA (ELEVATED LDL-C) (part 2)

items.title: Characteristics and Management of Homozygous FH (HoFH)
items.content: HoFH is suspected in patients with LDL >400 mg/dL, often presenting with cutaneous xanthomas or ASCVD. Treatment includes aggressive lipid-lowering strategies, including MTP inhibitors, antisense oligonucleotides to apoB, ANGPLT3 antibodies, and potentially liver transplantation.
keyPoints: Suspected in LDL >400 mg/dL without secondary cause
Cutaneous xanthomas or ASCVD indicative of HoFH
Aggressive treatment: MTP inhibitors, antisense oligonucleotides to apoB, ANGPLT3 antibodies
Consider liver transplantation as a last resort
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · HYPERCHOLESTEROLEMIA (ELEVATED LDL-C) (part 2)

items.title: Understanding Autosomal Recessive Hypercholesterolemia
items.content: Autosomal recessive hypercholesterolemia (ARH) is a rare disorder caused by mutations in the LDLRAP1 gene, affecting LDL receptor-mediated endocytosis. This results in elevated LDL-C levels, tendon xanthomas, and premature coronary artery disease.
keyPoints: Caused by mutations in LDLRAP1
LDL binds to receptors but is not internalized
Plasma LDL-C levels are intermediate between FH homozygotes and heterozygotes
CAD usually symptomatic in the third decade
LDL receptor function is normal or modestly reduced in fibroblasts, negligible in liver
Responds to statins but often requires additional therapy
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · HYPERCHOLESTEROLEMIA (ELEVATED LDL-C) (part 3)

items.title: Diagnosing and Managing Sitosterolemia
items.content: Sitosterolemia is caused by mutations in ABCG5 or ABCG8, leading to increased absorption and decreased excretion of plant sterols. This results in severe hypercholesterolemia, tendon xanthomas, premature ASCVD, anisocytosis, poikilocytosis, megathrombocytes, hemolysis, and splenomegaly.
keyPoints: Caused by loss-of-function mutations in ABCG5 or ABCG8
Increased intestinal absorption and reduced excretion of sterols
Transcriptional suppression of LDL receptor expression
Diagnosed by increased plasma sitosterol levels and gene sequencing
Diet, bile acid sequestrants, and cholesterol-absorption inhibitors are effective treatments
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · HYPERCHOLESTEROLEMIA (ELEVATED LDL-C) (part 3)

items.title: Recognizing Lysosomal Acid Lipase Deficiency
items.content: Lysosomal acid lipase deficiency (LALD) is an autosomal recessive disorder caused by mutations in the LIPA gene, leading to impaired hydrolysis of neutral lipids. This results in elevated LDL-C, low HDL-C, variable TG levels, and progressive fatty liver.
keyPoints: Caused by loss-of-function variants in LIPA
Impaired clearance of lipoproteins due to defective LAL enzyme
Characterized by elevated LDL-C, low HDL-C, and variable TGs
Progressive fatty liver leading to hepatic fibrosis
Severe form (Wolman's disease) presents in infancy and is rapidly fatal
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · HYPERCHOLESTEROLEMIA (ELEVATED LDL-C) (part 3)

items.title: Diagnosing Lysosomal Acid Lipase Deficiency (LALD)
items.content: LALD should be suspected in nonobese patients with elevated LDL-C, low HDL-C, and evidence of fatty liver without overt insulin resistance. Diagnosis involves a dried blood spot assay for LAL activity, DNA genotyping for common mutations, and potentially gene sequencing to identify additional mutations.
keyPoints: LALD is underdiagnosed but treatable with enzyme replacement therapy (sebelipase alfa).
Diagnosis confirmed by dried blood spot assay of LAL activity and DNA genotyping.
Liver biopsy assesses inflammation and fibrosis.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · HYPERCHOLESTEROLEMIA (ELEVATED LDL-C) (part 4)

items.title: Understanding Multifactorial Hypercholesterolemia
items.content: Most patients with elevated LDL-C have multifactorial etiology involving both genetic and environmental factors. Genetic variation accounts for at least 50% of LDL-C level variations, with polygenic hypercholesterolemia due to multiple common variants contributing modestly.
keyPoints: At least 50% of LDL-C variation is genetically determined.
Polygenic risk score can identify individuals with LDL levels similar to those with familial hypercholesterolemia (FH).
Diet, particularly saturated and trans fats, exacerbates LDL-C levels in genetically predisposed individuals.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · HYPERCHOLESTEROLEMIA (ELEVATED LDL-C) (part 4)

items.title: Understanding Mixed Hyperlipidemia: Definition and Risk
items.content: Mixed hyperlipidemia is characterized by fasting triglycerides (TGs) greater than 150 mg/dL and elevated cholesterol-containing lipoproteins, such as LDL-C over 130 mg/dL or non-HDL-C above 160 mg/dL. This condition is common due to genetic predisposition and environmental factors, significantly increasing the risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD).
keyPoints: Mixed hyperlipidemia defined by fasting TGs >150 mg/dL and LDL-C >130 mg/dL or non-HDL-C >160 mg/dL.
Common due to genetic predisposition and environmental factors.
Associated with elevated risk of ASCVD.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · MIXED HYPERLIPIDEMIA (ELEVATED TG AND LDL-C) (part 1)

items.title: Familial Dysbetalipoproteinemia: Genetic Basis and Presentation
items.content: Familial dysbetalipoproteinemia (FDBL), or type III hyperlipoproteinemia, is a recessive disorder caused by the apoE2 allele on both chromosomes. This condition results from reduced affinity of apoE2 for LDL receptors, leading to accumulation of remnant lipoproteins. It presents with hyperlipidemia, xanthomas, and premature ASCVD.
keyPoints: FDBL is caused by the presence of two copies of the apoE2 allele.
ApoE2 has a lower affinity for LDL receptors, causing accumulation of remnant lipoproteins.
Characterized by hyperlipidemia, xanthomas (tuberoeruptive and palmar), and premature ASCVD.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · MIXED HYPERLIPIDEMIA (ELEVATED TG AND LDL-C) (part 1)

items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Familial Dysbetalipoproteinemia
items.content: FDBL diagnosis can be confirmed by identifying very high levels of remnant lipoproteins or the apoE2/E2 genotype. The Friedewald formula is invalid for LDL-C calculation in FDBL due to altered VLDL composition. Management includes lifestyle modifications, statins as first-line therapy, and additional pharmacologic agents if needed.
keyPoints: Diagnosis via high remnant lipoprotein levels or apoE2/E2 genotype confirmation.
Friedewald formula invalid for LDL-C calculation in FDBL.
Management includes lifestyle changes, statins, cholesterol absorption inhibitors, PCSK9 inhibitors, and fibrates.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · MIXED HYPERLIPIDEMIA (ELEVATED TG AND LDL-C) (part 1)

items.title: Understanding Hepatic Lipase Deficiency
items.content: Hepatic lipase deficiency is a rare autosomal recessive disorder caused by biallelic loss-of-function mutations in LIPC, leading to elevated plasma levels of cholesterol and triglycerides due to accumulation of lipoprotein remnants. The association with ASCVD is not entirely clear.
keyPoints: Hepatic lipase deficiency is very rare and autosomal recessive.
Caused by biallelic loss-of-function mutations in LIPC.
Characterized by elevated plasma levels of cholesterol, triglycerides, and HDL-C.
Association with ASCVD is not entirely clear; anecdotal reports suggest possible premature CVD.
Statin therapy is recommended to reduce remnant lipoproteins and cardiovascular risk.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · MIXED HYPERLIPIDEMIA (ELEVATED TG AND LDL-C) (part 2)

items.title: Characteristics of Familial Combined Hyperlipidemia (FCHL)
items.content: FCHL is a common familial lipid disorder characterized by elevated plasma levels of triglycerides and LDL-C, often with reduced HDL-C. It significantly contributes to premature CHD, affecting ~1 in 100–200 individuals.
keyPoints: Occurs in ~1 in 100–200 individuals.
Characterized by elevations in TGs (VLDL) and LDL-C, with reduced HDL-C levels.
~20% of patients who develop CHD under age 60 have FCHL.
Phenotypes include elevated LDL-C, elevated TGs due to VLDL, or both.
Plasma apoB levels are substantially elevated, indicating small dense LDL particles.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · MIXED HYPERLIPIDEMIA (ELEVATED TG AND LDL-C) (part 2)

items.title: Diagnosis and Management of FCHL
items.content: FCHL diagnosis is suggested by mixed dyslipidemia with specific plasma levels and family history. Aggressive treatment, including lifestyle changes and lipid-lowering drugs like high-intensity statins, ezetimibe, PCSK9 inhibitors, or bempedoic acid, is recommended.
keyPoints: Mixed dyslipidemia: TG levels 150-500 mg/dL, total cholesterol 200-400 mg/dL, HDL-C <40 mg/dL in men and <50 mg/dL in women.
Family history of mixed dyslipidemia or premature CHD suggests FCHL.
Plasma apoB levels can support diagnosis if substantially elevated relative to LDL-C.
Aggressive treatment is necessary due to increased risk of premature CHD.
High-intensity statins are first-line; combination therapy may be needed.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · MIXED HYPERLIPIDEMIA (ELEVATED TG AND LDL-C) (part 2)

items.subtopic: SECONDARY CONTRIBUTORS TO ELEVATED LEVELS OF APOB-CONTAINING LIPOPROTEINS (part 1)
items.title: Impact of High-Carbohydrate Diet on TG Levels
items.content: A high-carbohydrate diet leads to increased hepatic VLDL-TG secretion, elevating triglyceride levels. This occurs as dietary carbohydrates are converted into fatty acids in the liver, which are then esterified and secreted as VLDL.
keyPoints: Excessive carbohydrate intake increases hepatic VLDL-TG secretion.
Reduction in carbohydrate consumption can substantially reduce TG levels.
Replacing carbohydrates with saturated fat may elevate LDL-C levels.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · SECONDARY CONTRIBUTORS TO ELEVATED LEVELS OF APOB-CONTAINING LIPOPROTEINS (part 1)

items.subtopic: SECONDARY CONTRIBUTORS TO ELEVATED LEVELS OF APOB-CONTAINING LIPOPROTEINS (part 1)
items.title: Role of Obesity and Insulin Resistance in Dyslipidemia
items.content: Obesity, insulin resistance, and type 2 diabetes primarily affect triglycerides by increasing hepatic VLDL production. This is due to increased free fatty acid delivery from adipose tissue to the liver and reduced LPL activity.
keyPoints: Obesity leads to excessive hepatic VLDL production.
Insulin resistance reduces LPL activity, worsening hypertriglyceridemia.
Dyslipidemia in this context is often associated with low HDL-C levels.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · SECONDARY CONTRIBUTORS TO ELEVATED LEVELS OF APOB-CONTAINING LIPOPROTEINS (part 1)

items.subtopic: SECONDARY CONTRIBUTORS TO ELEVATED LEVELS OF APOB-CONTAINING LIPOPROTEINS (part 1)
items.title: Chronic Kidney Disease and Hypertriglyceridemia
items.content: Chronic kidney disease is associated with mild hypertriglyceridemia due to reduced TG lipolysis and remnant clearance. Patients often require lipid-lowering agents, especially statins.
keyPoints: CKD leads to mild HTG (150–400 mg/dL) from VLDL accumulation.
TG lipolysis and remnant clearance are impaired in renal failure.
Statins are recommended for lipid management in CKD patients.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · SECONDARY CONTRIBUTORS TO ELEVATED LEVELS OF APOB-CONTAINING LIPOPROTEINS (part 1)

items.subtopic: SECONDARY CONTRIBUTORS TO ELEVATED LEVELS OF APOB-CONTAINING LIPOPROTEINS (part 2)
items.title: Hypothyroidism and Elevated LDL-C Levels
items.content: Hypothyroidism is a significant medical condition that leads to elevated LDL-C levels due to the downregulation of hepatic LDL receptors, which are normally upregulated by thyroid hormones. Patients with unexplained increases in LDL-C should be screened for hypothyroidism using TSH measurements.
keyPoints: Hypothyroidism is a major cause of elevated plasma LDL-C levels.
Elevated LDL-C results from downregulation of hepatic LDL receptors.
Screen patients with unexplained LDL-C elevation for hypothyroidism by measuring TSH.
Thyroid replacement therapy usually reduces LDL-C, indicating primary lipoprotein disorder if not effective.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · SECONDARY CONTRIBUTORS TO ELEVATED LEVELS OF APOB-CONTAINING LIPOPROTEINS (part 2)

items.subtopic: SECONDARY CONTRIBUTORS TO ELEVATED LEVELS OF APOB-CONTAINING LIPOPROTEINS (part 2)
items.title: Nephrotic Syndrome and Dyslipidemia
items.content: Nephrotic syndrome leads to excessive VLDL production, causing elevated levels of both TGs and LDL-C. The exact mechanism is unclear but may involve hypoalbuminemia-induced increased hepatic protein synthesis. Lipid-lowering therapy with statins is often required for chronic cases.
keyPoints: Nephrotic syndrome causes excessive VLDL production, elevating TGs and LDL-C.
The molecular mechanism involves hypoalbuminemia leading to increased hepatic protein synthesis.
Treatment of underlying renal disease may normalize lipid profiles.
Chronic nephrotic syndrome often requires statin therapy for lipid management.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · SECONDARY CONTRIBUTORS TO ELEVATED LEVELS OF APOB-CONTAINING LIPOPROTEINS (part 2)

items.subtopic: DISORDERS ASSOCIATED WITH REDUCED APOB-CONTAINING LIPOPROTEINS (part 1)
items.title: Abetalipoproteinemia: Recognizing and Managing a Rare Disorder
items.content: Abetalipoproteinemia is an autosomal recessive disorder caused by mutations in the MTTP gene, leading to severe deficiencies in apoB-containing lipoproteins. This results in extremely low plasma cholesterol and triglyceride levels, with undetectable chylomicrons, VLDLs, LDLs, and apoB.
keyPoints: Plasma concentrations of LDL-C <60 mg/dL suggest an inherited condition like abetalipoproteinemia if the individual is otherwise healthy.
Abetalipoproteinemia presents with symptoms such as diarrhea, failure to thrive in early childhood, and progressive neurologic and visual impairments.
Deficiencies in fat-soluble vitamins (E, A, K) are due to impaired absorption and transport, necessitating a low-fat, high-caloric, vitamin-enriched diet with large doses of vitamin E.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · DISORDERS ASSOCIATED WITH REDUCED APOB-CONTAINING LIPOPROTEINS (part 1)

items.subtopic: DISORDERS ASSOCIATED WITH REDUCED APOB-CONTAINING LIPOPROTEINS (part 1)
items.title: Familial Hypobetalipoproteinemia: Understanding Genetic Impact on Lipoproteins
items.content: FHBL is characterized by low total cholesterol, LDL-C, and apoB due to mutations in the APOB gene. These mutations often result in a truncated apoB protein, impairing chylomicron and VLDL assembly and secretion.
keyPoints: Heterozygous FHBL typically presents with LDL-C levels <60–80 mg/dL and low plasma triglycerides.
Patients may have elevated hepatic fat and liver transaminases but rarely develop significant hepatic inflammation or fibrosis.
Truncating mutations in both apoB alleles cause homozygous FHBL, a rare disorder with nearly undetectable LDL-C and apoB.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · DISORDERS ASSOCIATED WITH REDUCED APOB-CONTAINING LIPOPROTEINS (part 1)

items.subtopic: DISORDERS ASSOCIATED WITH REDUCED APOB-CONTAINING LIPOPROTEINS (part 1)
items.title: Distinguishing Abetalipoproteinemia from Homozygous Hypobetalipoproteinemia
items.content: While both conditions present with very low LDL-C and apoB, they differ in inheritance patterns and severity of neurologic defects. Abetalipoproteinemia is recessive, whereas homozygous hypobetalipoproteinemia is co-dominant.
keyPoints: In abetalipoproteinemia, parents (obligate heterozygotes) have normal plasma lipid levels; in homozygous hypobetalipoproteinemia, they have low LDL-C and apoB.
Neurologic defects are similar but less severe in homozygous hypobetalipoproteinemia compared to abetalipoproteinemia.
Genetic testing can help distinguish between these conditions based on inheritance patterns.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · DISORDERS ASSOCIATED WITH REDUCED APOB-CONTAINING LIPOPROTEINS (part 1)

items.subtopic: DISORDERS ASSOCIATED WITH REDUCED APOB-CONTAINING LIPOPROTEINS (part 2)
items.title: Familial PCSK9 Deficiency and its Impact on LDL-C Levels
items.content: Familial PCSK9 deficiency results from loss-of-function mutations in the PCSK9 gene, leading to increased hepatic LDL receptor activity and reduced plasma levels of LDL-C. This condition is more common among individuals of African descent.
keyPoints: Heterozygous nonsense mutations in PCSK9 result in a ~30–40% reduction in plasma LDL-C levels.
Individuals with these mutations have substantial protection from coronary heart disease (CHD).
Homozygotes for these mutations can have extremely low LDL-C levels (<20 mg/dL) and appear otherwise healthy.
The R46L mutation, more common in individuals of European descent, leads to a ~15–20% reduction in plasma LDL-C levels and a 45% reduction in CHD risk.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · DISORDERS ASSOCIATED WITH REDUCED APOB-CONTAINING LIPOPROTEINS (part 2)

items.subtopic: DISORDERS ASSOCIATED WITH REDUCED APOB-CONTAINING LIPOPROTEINS (part 2)
items.title: Familial Combined Hypolipidemia due to ANGPTL3 Deficiency
items.content: Mutations in both alleles of the ANGPTL3 gene lead to familial combined hypolipidemia, characterized by low levels of TG, LDL-C, and HDL-C. This condition results from increased lipoprotein lipase (LPL) activity due to ANGPTL3 deficiency.
keyPoints: ANGPTL3 inhibits LPL, delaying clearance of triglyceride-rich lipoproteins (TRLs).
Deficiency in ANGPTL3 raises LPL activity and lowers blood TG levels while also lowering LDL-C and raising HDL-C levels.
This condition is associated with a reduced risk for coronary heart disease (CHD).
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · DISORDERS ASSOCIATED WITH REDUCED APOB-CONTAINING LIPOPROTEINS (part 2)

items.subtopic: DISORDERS ASSOCIATED WITH REDUCED HIGH-DENSITY LIPOPROTEINS
items.title: Clinical Relevance of Low HDL-C Levels in ASCVD Risk Assessment
items.content: Low levels of high-density lipoprotein cholesterol (HDL-C) are significant predictors of increased cardiovascular risk, particularly atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD). Despite this association, low HDL-C is not considered directly causal for ASCVD development. Therefore, while it remains an important biomarker in cardiovascular risk assessment, raising HDL-C levels therapeutically to reduce cardiovascular risk is no longer recommended.
keyPoints: Low HDL-C defined as <50 mg/dL in women and <40 mg/dL in men
Independent predictor of increased cardiovascular risk
Used in standardized risk calculators for ASCVD assessment
Not considered a direct causal factor for ASCVD development
HDL-C is not a target for therapeutic intervention to reduce cardiovascular risk
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · DISORDERS ASSOCIATED WITH REDUCED HIGH-DENSITY LIPOPROTEINS

items.subtopic: DISORDERS ASSOCIATED WITH REDUCED HIGH-DENSITY LIPOPROTEINS
items.title: Factors Influencing HDL Metabolism and Low HDL-C Levels
items.content: HDL metabolism is influenced by triglyceride (TG) metabolism, insulin resistance, and inflammation. Most patients with low HDL-C have a combination of genetic predisposition and secondary factors such as obesity and insulin resistance. Elevated TGs often lead to reduced HDL-C levels due to the interplay between triglyceride-rich lipoproteins (TRLs) and HDL. Accelerated catabolism of HDL and its associated apoA-I protein is a common physiological basis for low HDL-C in these patients.
keyPoints: HDL metabolism influenced by TG metabolism, insulin resistance, inflammation
Low HDL-C often due to genetic predisposition and secondary factors like obesity, insulin resistance
Elevated TGs commonly result in reduced HDL-C levels
Accelerated catabolism of HDL and apoA-I protein is a common cause for low HDL-C
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · DISORDERS ASSOCIATED WITH REDUCED HIGH-DENSITY LIPOPROTEINS

items.title: Role of APOA1 in HDL Synthesis and Clinical Implications
items.content: Mutations in the APOA1 gene, such as deletions or missense mutations like Arg173Cys ($ _{Milano} $), lead to significantly reduced HDL-C levels. Complete deletion results in no circulating apoA-I and can cause cholesterol deposits and premature CHD. Heterozygotes show low HDL-C but typically lack clinical sequelae.
keyPoints: Complete APOA1 gene deletion leads to absence of circulating HDL, critical for biogenesis.
Arg173Cys mutation in apoA-I ($ _{Milano} $) results in HDL-C <25 mg/dL with no increased CHD risk.
Heterozygotes for APOA1 mutations have reduced HDL-C but usually no clinical sequelae.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · PRIMARY (GENETIC) CAUSES OF LOW HDL-C (part 1)

items.title: Tangier Disease and ABCA1 Deficiency
items.content: Tangier disease, caused by mutations in the ABCA1 gene, results in extremely low HDL-C (<5 mg/dL) and apoA-I levels. It leads to cholesterol accumulation in reticuloendothelial system tissues, causing hepatosplenomegaly and characteristic tonsil changes. Heterozygotes show moderately reduced HDL-C (~15–40 mg/dL).
keyPoints: Tangier disease results from ABCA1 mutations leading to HDL-C <5 mg/dL.
Cholesterol accumulates in reticuloendothelial system, causing hepatosplenomegaly and enlarged tonsils.
Heterozygotes have moderately reduced HDL-C (~15–40 mg/dL).
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · PRIMARY (GENETIC) CAUSES OF LOW HDL-C (part 1)

items.title: Familial LCAT Deficiency and Its Clinical Manifestations
items.content: Mutations in the LCAT gene cause familial LCAT deficiency, leading to increased free cholesterol in lipoproteins. This results in very low HDL-C levels (<10 mg/dL) and can cause corneal opacification, HTG, hemolytic anemia, and renal insufficiency in complete deficiency. Despite low HDL-C, ASCVD is not a consistent feature.
keyPoints: Familial LCAT deficiency leads to increased free cholesterol (>70% of total plasma cholesterol).
HDL-C levels are very low (<10 mg/dL) with corneal opacification and HTG.
Complete deficiency causes hemolytic anemia and renal insufficiency, but not consistent ASCVD.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · PRIMARY (GENETIC) CAUSES OF LOW HDL-C (part 1)

items.title: Identifying Primary Hypoalphalipoproteinemia
items.content: Primary hypoalphalipoproteinemia is characterized by a plasma HDL-C level below the tenth percentile, with normal cholesterol and triglyceride levels, no secondary causes of low HDL-C, and absence of clinical signs of LCAT deficiency or Tangier disease. A family history may suggest an inherited condition.
keyPoints: Plasma HDL-C level below the tenth percentile
Normal cholesterol and triglyceride levels
No apparent secondary causes
No clinical signs of LCAT deficiency or Tangier disease
Family history suggests Mendelian inheritance
Most cases likely have a polygenic etiology
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · PRIMARY (GENETIC) CAUSES OF LOW HDL-C (part 2) | SECONDARY FACTORS THAT REDUCE HDL-C LEVELS

items.subtopic: SECONDARY FACTORS THAT REDUCE HDL-C LEVELS
items.title: Impact of Anabolic Steroids and Testosterone on HDL-C Levels
items.content: Anabolic steroids significantly lower HDL-C levels, while testosterone supplementation also reduces HDL-C but to a lesser extent. A detailed history of medication and supplement use is crucial in young males with unexplained low HDL-C.
keyPoints: Anabolic steroids dramatically lower HDL-C
Testosterone reduces HDL-C less than anabolic steroids
Unexplained very low HDL-C warrants investigation into steroid or testosterone use
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · PRIMARY (GENETIC) CAUSES OF LOW HDL-C (part 2) | SECONDARY FACTORS THAT REDUCE HDL-C LEVELS

items.title: Systematic Screening and Management of Dyslipidemia
items.content: Physicians should systematically screen for dyslipidemia due to its high prevalence and the clinical benefits of early diagnosis. This includes ruling out secondary causes, suspecting inherited disorders, promoting family-based screening, assessing ASCVD risk, and considering additional risk stratification approaches.
keyPoints: High prevalence of dyslipidemia
Proven clinical benefits of early diagnosis and therapy initiation
Screen systematically for lipids
Rule out secondary causes of dyslipidemia
Suspect inherited disorders where appropriate
Promote family-based screening
Assess risk for ASCVD
Consider additional risk stratification approaches
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · Lipoprotein Disorders

items.title: Role of Genomic Medicine in Lipoprotein Disorders
items.content: The field of clinical lipidology is advancing with the integration of genomic medicine. Diagnostic DNA sequencing or genotyping can enhance molecular diagnosis, facilitate appropriate therapeutic interventions, and promote family-based cascade screening for disorders like FCS, FPLD, FH, and FDBL.
keyPoints: Advancement in clinical lipidology with genomic medicine
Diagnostic DNA sequencing/genotyping enhances molecular diagnosis
Facilitates appropriate therapeutic interventions
Promotes family-based cascade screening based on genetic diagnosis
Relevant for disorders like FCS, FPLD, FH, and FDBL
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · Lipoprotein Disorders

items.title: Identifying Severe Hypertriglyceridemia Risk Factors
items.content: Severe hypertriglyceridemia (HTG) is diagnosed when fasting plasma triglyceride levels exceed 500 mg/dL, posing a risk for pancreatitis. If TG levels are persistently above 1000 mg/dL with a total cholesterol-to-TG ratio greater than 8, familial chylomicron syndrome (FCS) should be considered, prompting genetic testing of an FCS gene panel.
keyPoints: Severe HTG is defined as fasting TG >500 mg/dL.
Risk for pancreatitis increases with severe HTG.
TG levels >1000 mg/dL and cholesterol-to-TG ratio >8 suggest FCS; consider genetic testing of an FCS gene panel.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Diagnosing Severe Hypercholesterolemia and Familial Hypercholesterolemia (FH)
items.content: Severe hypercholesterolemia is indicated by LDL-C levels >190 mg/dL, increasing the risk for premature atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD). In the absence of secondary causes, FH should be considered, especially with a family history of hypercholesterolemia or premature coronary heart disease. Genetic testing may confirm FH.
keyPoints: Severe hypercholesterolemia is defined as LDL-C >190 mg/dL.
FH consideration is crucial if there's a relevant family history and absence of secondary causes.
Genetic testing for FH can lead to earlier, more aggressive treatment.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Approach to Mixed Hyperlipidemia Diagnosis
items.content: Mixed hyperlipidemia is characterized by elevated fasting plasma levels of both triglycerides (>150 mg/dL) and LDL-C (>130 mg/dL), often with reduced HDL-C (<40 mg/dL in men, <50 mg/dL in women). This condition increases ASCVD risk. Elevated apoB levels can indicate familial combined hyperlipidemia (FCHL), necessitating aggressive treatment.
keyPoints: Mixed hyperlipidemia involves TG >150 mg/dL and LDL-C >130 mg/dL, often with low HDL-C.
Increased ASCVD risk warrants lifestyle and pharmacologic interventions.
Elevated apoB levels suggest FCHL; these patients require aggressive treatment.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Severe Hypertriglyceridemia | LIFESTYLE AND MODIFIABLE FACTORS
items.title: Why Lifestyle Modifications Are Crucial for Severe HTG Management
items.content: Lifestyle changes can significantly reduce plasma triglyceride (TG) levels in patients with severe hypertriglyceridemia. Key interventions include dietary adjustments, increased physical activity, and weight management.
keyPoints: Encourage reduction or elimination of alcohol intake to lower TG levels.
Dietary consultation is beneficial; restrict dietary fat and simple carbohydrates to reduce chylomicron formation and hepatic TG production.
Aerobic exercise and regular physical activity can positively impact TG levels.
Weight loss, including bariatric surgery in extreme cases, can substantially reduce plasma TG levels.
Better control of diabetes through medication like GLP-1 agonists can lower TGs.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · Severe Hypertriglyceridemia | LIFESTYLE AND MODIFIABLE FACTORS | PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY

items.title: When to Initiate Pharmacologic Therapy for Severe HTG
items.content: Pharmacologic therapy is recommended for patients with severe hypertriglyceridemia who have fasting TG levels >500 mg/dL despite lifestyle interventions.
keyPoints: Fibrates reduce TG levels by ~30% and are often first-line therapy but may modestly raise LDL-C and cause myopathy, especially when combined with statins.
Omega-3 fatty acids (fish oils) lower fasting TG levels by ~30% in doses of 3–4 g/d; they can increase plasma LDL-C and prolong bleeding time.
Statins are used to reduce both plasma TG levels and ASCVD risk in patients at increased risk.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · Severe Hypertriglyceridemia | LIFESTYLE AND MODIFIABLE FACTORS | PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY

items.title: Emerging Therapies for Severe HTG: APOC3 Silencing
items.content: APOC3 silencing, using agents like Volanesorsen, is an emerging therapy that significantly reduces plasma apoC-III and TG levels in patients with familial chylomicronemia syndrome (FCS).
keyPoints: Volanesorsen targets APOC3 mRNA to reduce TG levels.
It is approved in Europe for FCS but associated with severe thrombocytopenia.
Additional therapies targeting APOC3 and other genes like ANGPTL3 are under development.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · Severe Hypertriglyceridemia | LIFESTYLE AND MODIFIABLE FACTORS | PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY

items.subtopic: Hypercholesterolemia (Elevated LDL-C with or without Elevated TG) | LIFESTYLE AND MODIFIABLE FACTORS
items.title: Importance of Reducing LDL-C in Hypercholesterolemia for ASCVD Risk Reduction
items.content: Intervention to reduce LDL-C significantly lowers the risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD), including myocardial infarction and stroke, as well as total mortality. Patients with hypercholesterolemia should be assessed for cardiovascular risk and need for intervention, even if their LDL-C levels are within the normal range.
keyPoints: Reducing LDL-C substantially reduces ASCVD risk, including myocardial infarction and stroke.
Patients at high risk for ASCVD benefit from LDL-C reduction even with 'normal' LDL-C levels.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · Hypercholesterolemia (Elevated LDL-C with or without Elevated TG) | LIFESTYLE AND MODIFIABLE FACTORS

items.subtopic: Hypercholesterolemia (Elevated LDL-C with or without Elevated TG) | LIFESTYLE AND MODIFIABLE FACTORS
items.title: Role of Lifestyle Modifications in Managing Elevated LDL-C
items.content: Lifestyle modifications can help manage elevated LDL-C levels, though they are generally less effective than in hypertriglyceridemia (HTG). Dietary counseling to reduce saturated and trans fats is recommended. Weight loss for obese patients and optimal control of hypothyroidism are also advised.
keyPoints: Dietary changes can reduce saturated and trans fat intake, aiding LDL-C management.
Weight loss is beneficial for obese patients with elevated LDL-C.
Regular aerobic exercise has limited impact on LDL-C but offers cardiovascular benefits.
Optimal control of hypothyroidism can help manage LDL-C levels.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · Hypercholesterolemia (Elevated LDL-C with or without Elevated TG) | LIFESTYLE AND MODIFIABLE FACTORS

items.title: When to Initiate Statin Therapy
items.content: Statin therapy is recommended for patients with established ASCVD or those at high cardiovascular risk. In the absence of ASCVD, statins are advised for patients over 40 years old with a >7.5% 10-year CVD risk and for younger individuals based on lifetime risk assessment.
keyPoints: Statin therapy is first-line treatment for LDL-C reduction in patients with ASCVD or high cardiovascular risk.
For patients without ASCVD, statins are recommended if the 10-year CVD risk >7.5% using the ASCVD pooled cohort risk calculator.
In younger patients, assess lifetime risk of CVD and family history to guide statin initiation.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY (part 1)

items.title: Mechanism and Efficacy of Statins
items.content: Statins inhibit HMG-CoA reductase, leading to increased LDL receptor expression and reduced plasma LDL-C levels. They significantly lower major cardiovascular events in both primary and secondary prevention.
keyPoints: Statins reduce plasma LDL-C by 30–55%, with a ~6% further reduction when the dose is doubled.
The most important side effect of statin therapy is muscle pain, occurring in 3–5% of patients.
Statins are associated with a slight increase in incident type 2 diabetes but offer significant cardiovascular benefits.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY (part 1)

items.title: Safety and Monitoring of Statin Therapy
items.content: Statins are safe and well-tolerated, with muscle pain being the most common side effect. Routine monitoring of serum CK is not necessary unless symptoms occur. Mild liver enzyme elevation may occur but usually does not require discontinuation.
keyPoints: Muscle pain (myalgia) occurs in 3–5% of patients on statins, with severe myopathy being rare.
Risk factors for statin-associated myopathy include older age, renal insufficiency, and certain drug interactions.
Elevation in liver transaminases is usually mild and transient; routine monitoring is not required.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY (part 1)

items.title: Ezetimibe as a Second-Line Therapy for LDL-C Reduction
items.content: Ezetimibe inhibits cholesterol absorption by binding to NPC1L1, reducing plasma LDL-C levels by about 18% when used alone. Its effect is additive with statins and significantly reduces cardiovascular events compared to statin therapy alone.
keyPoints: Inhibits cholesterol absorption by ~60%
Reduces plasma LDL-C by ~18% when taken as monotherapy
Additive effect with statins, further reducing major cardiovascular events
Second-line option for additional LDL-C reduction in patients on statin therapy
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY (part 2)

items.title: PCSK9 Inhibitors' Role in LDL-C Management and Cardiovascular Risk Reduction
items.content: PCSK9 inhibitors, by increasing the number of available LDL receptors, reduce LDL-C levels by ~60% and modestly lower Lp(a) levels. They are effective as second- or third-line therapy for patients with familial hypercholesterolemia (FH) or atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD) not achieving target LDL-C levels on statins alone.
keyPoints: Reduces LDL-C by ~60%
Significantly reduces cardiovascular events when added to statin therapy
Administered subcutaneously every 2 weeks
Used as second- or third-line therapy in specific patient populations
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY (part 2)

items.title: Bempedoic Acid for Statin-Intolerant Patients and LDL-C Reduction
items.content: Bempedoic acid, a novel ACL inhibitor, reduces LDL-C by ~18% when added to statins and by ~23% as monotherapy. It is well tolerated in patients with statin-induced myalgias and significantly reduces major adverse cardiovascular events in statin-intolerant individuals.
keyPoints: Reduces LDL-C by ~18% when combined with a statin
~23% reduction as monotherapy
Significantly reduces major adverse cardiovascular events by 13%
Well tolerated, especially in patients with statin-induced myalgias
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY (part 2)

items.title: Mechanism of Action of Bile Acid Sequestrants
items.content: Bile acid sequestrants (BAS) bind bile acids in the intestine, promoting their excretion and reducing reabsorption. This diversion leads to increased hepatic conversion of cholesterol into bile acids, upregulating LDL receptors and enhancing LDL clearance from plasma.
keyPoints: BAS include cholestyramine, colestipol, and colesevelam.
Primarily reduce plasma LDL-C levels.
Can increase plasma triglycerides (TGs).
Not recommended for patients with hypertriglyceridemia (HTG) due to potential TG increase.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY (part 3)

items.title: Clinical Use and Side Effects of Bile Acid Sequestrants
items.content: BAS are effective in combination with statins or ezetimibe for lowering LDL-C. They cause gastrointestinal side effects like bloating and constipation but are safe due to lack of systemic absorption.
keyPoints: Cholestyramine and colestipol require suspension in liquids; colesevelam is tablet form.
Colesevelam requires up to six to seven tablets per day for effective LDL-C lowering.
Side effects are limited to the gastrointestinal tract.
BAS are very safe as they are not systemically absorbed.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY (part 3)

items.question: What role does apoE play in lipoprotein metabolism?
options: It provides structural support to LDL particles.
It regulates the metabolism and clearance of TG-rich particles.
It is involved in the synthesis of VLDLs.
It is a major component of chylomicrons.

Transports free fatty acids to tissues

items.subtopic: HDL METABOLISM AND REVERSE CHOLESTEROL TRANSPORT

items.subtopic: HDL METABOLISM AND REVERSE CHOLESTEROL TRANSPORT
items.question: What is the primary role of lecithin-cholesterol acyltransferase (LCAT) in HDL metabolism?
options: Transfers phospholipids among different classes of HDL particles.
Hydrolyzes triglycerides and phospholipids to generate smaller HDL particles.
Esterifies cholesterol within the HDL particle, forming cholesteryl ester.
Facilitates selective uptake of HDL CE by hepatocytes.

options: It cannot be used if the triglyceride level exceeds ~200 mg/dL.
It requires direct measurement of apoB levels for accuracy.
It cannot be used if the triglyceride level is >400 mg/dL.
It does not account for cholesterol in VLDL and IDL particles.

options: Non-HDL-C includes cholesterol from all lipoproteins, including VLDL and IDL.
Non-HDL-C provides a direct measurement of apoB levels.
Non-HDL-C is unaffected by triglyceride levels.
Non-HDL-C calculation requires fasting plasma samples.
items.explanation: Non-HDL-C incorporates cholesterol from VLDL and IDL, which are also atherogenic.

To substantially reduce the risk of ASCVD

Receptor-mediated uptake of apoB-containing lipoproteins by the liver
Cellular cholesterol metabolism in hepatocytes and enterocytes

items.explanation: FCS is primarily caused by a deficiency in LPL, which is required for the hydrolysis of TGs in chylomicrons and VLDLs.

items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · SEVERE HYPERTRIGLYCERIDEMIA (part 1)

items.question: What is the primary therapeutic goal in managing severe hypertriglyceridemia to prevent pancreatitis?
options: Reduce fasting TG levels to <500 mg/dL
Increase dietary fat intake to >30 g/day
Administer fish oils as first-line therapy
items.explanation: The primary goal is to reduce fasting TG levels to <500 mg/dL to prevent pancreatitis.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · SEVERE HYPERTRIGLYCERIDEMIA (part 2)

items.question: Which dietary intervention is essential in managing familial chylomicronemia syndrome (FCS)?
options: Increase fat-soluble vitamin supplementation without dietary changes
Markedly restrict dietary fat intake, often to as little as 15 g/day
Prescribe fish oils or fibrates as primary treatment
items.explanation: Dietary management includes markedly restricting fat intake to control chylomicronemia.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · SEVERE HYPERTRIGLYCERIDEMIA (part 2)

items.question: What genetic approach is promising for treating severe hypertriglyceridemia?
options: Silencing of APOC3 or ANGPTL3 in the liver
Administering exogenous apoC-II without genetic intervention

items.question: What condition can be managed with recombinant leptin administration?
items.explanation: Recombinant leptin administration is effective in treating congenital generalized lipodystrophy.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · SEVERE HYPERTRIGLYCERIDEMIA (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a characteristic feature of multifactorial hypertriglyceridemia?

items.explanation: Management is mostly focused on reducing TGs to prevent pancreatitis.

options: Diet high in saturated and trans fats
High intake of omega-3 fatty acids
items.explanation: A diet high in saturated and trans fats reduces LDL receptor activity.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · HYPERCHOLESTEROLEMIA (ELEVATED LDL-C) (part 1)

items.question: What is the estimated population frequency of heterozygous FH?

items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · HYPERCHOLESTEROLEMIA (ELEVATED LDL-C) (part 1)

items.question: Which condition is characterized by elevated plasma levels of LDL-C and relatively normal TG levels?

items.question: Which of the following is considered a secondary cause that should be excluded when diagnosing Familial Hypercholesterolemia (FH)?

items.question: What is a common clinical presentation in childhood for receptor-negative HoFH patients?

items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · HYPERCHOLESTEROLEMIA (ELEVATED LDL-C) (part 2)

items.question: Which gene mutation causes Autosomal Recessive Hypercholesterolemia (ARH)?

items.question: What is the primary cause of elevated LDL-C levels in Lysosomal Acid Lipase Deficiency (LALD)?

options: Diet has no effect on LDL-C levels.
Diet can exacerbate elevated LDL-C levels.
Diet reduces the impact of genetic factors on LDL-C.
Diet only affects HDL-C, not LDL-C.

items.question: What is a common precipitating factor for the development of overt dysbetalipoproteinemia in apoE2/E2 homozygotes?

items.question: Which treatment strategy is recommended for patients with familial combined hyperlipidemia to reduce cardiovascular risk?

Mutations causing complete loss of PCSK9 function

items.statement: Most individuals with severe hypertriglyceridemia have a single-gene disorder.
items.explanation: Most individuals with severe hypertriglyceridemia have increased polygenic risk, often exacerbated by secondary factors.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Familial combined hyperlipidemia can be diagnosed by measuring substantially elevated apoB levels.
items.explanation: Elevated apoB levels help identify patients with familial combined hyperlipidemia, who are at increased risk of ASCVD.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Severe Hypertriglyceridemia | LIFESTYLE AND MODIFIABLE FACTORS | PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY
items.statement: Omega-3 fatty acids can reduce fasting TG levels by approximately 30%.
items.explanation: n-3 PUFAs in doses of 3–4 g/d are effective at lowering fasting TG levels by ~30%.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · Severe Hypertriglyceridemia | LIFESTYLE AND MODIFIABLE FACTORS | PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY

items.subtopic: Severe Hypertriglyceridemia | LIFESTYLE AND MODIFIABLE FACTORS | PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY
items.statement: Statins are the first-line pharmacologic therapy for severe hypertriglyceridemia.
items.explanation: Fibrates, not statins, are often used as first-line therapy for severe hypertriglyceridemia.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · Severe Hypertriglyceridemia | LIFESTYLE AND MODIFIABLE FACTORS | PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY

items.subtopic: Hypercholesterolemia (Elevated LDL-C with or without Elevated TG) | LIFESTYLE AND MODIFIABLE FACTORS
items.statement: Patients with hypercholesterolemia should be assessed for cardiovascular risk and need for intervention.
items.explanation: It is imperative that patients with hypercholesterolemia be carefully assessed for cardiovascular risk and need for intervention.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · Hypercholesterolemia (Elevated LDL-C with or without Elevated TG) | LIFESTYLE AND MODIFIABLE FACTORS

items.subtopic: Hypercholesterolemia (Elevated LDL-C with or without Elevated TG) | LIFESTYLE AND MODIFIABLE FACTORS
items.statement: Regular aerobic exercise significantly reduces plasma LDL-C levels.
items.explanation: Regular aerobic exercise has relatively little impact on reducing plasma LDL-C levels.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · Hypercholesterolemia (Elevated LDL-C with or without Elevated TG) | LIFESTYLE AND MODIFIABLE FACTORS

items.statement: Statin therapy is associated with a significant increase in total mortality.
items.explanation: Statins significantly reduce major cardiovascular events and, in some cases, total mortality.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY (part 1)

items.statement: Routine monitoring of serum CK levels is necessary for all patients on statin therapy.
items.explanation: Serum CK levels do not need to be monitored routinely unless there are symptoms, as elevated CK without symptoms does not predict myopathy.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY (part 1)

items.statement: Ezetimibe significantly reduces triglyceride (TG) and HDL-C levels.
items.explanation: The effects of ezetimibe on TG and HDL-C levels are negligible.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY (part 2)

items.statement: Bempedoic acid is associated with an increased incidence of diabetes.
items.explanation: Unlike statins, bempedoic acid is not associated with an increased incidence of diabetes.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY (part 2)

items.statement: Bile acid sequestrants are effective in combination with statins.
items.explanation: BAS are effective in combination with statins and in combination with ezetimibe.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY (part 3)

items.statement: The primary side effects of bile acid sequestrants include systemic toxicity.
items.explanation: Side effects of resins are limited to the gastrointestinal tract and include bloating and constipation.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY (part 3)

items.subtopic: TRANSPORT OF INTESTINALLY DERIVED DIETARY LIPIDS BY CHYLOMICRONS
items.assertion: Chylomicrons are secreted directly into the systemic circulation.
items.reason: Nascent chylomicrons are first secreted into the intestinal lymph and then delivered to the systemic circulation via the thoracic duct.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because chylomicrons are not secreted directly into the systemic circulation; they pass through the intestinal lymph and thoracic duct first.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · TRANSPORT OF INTESTINALLY DERIVED DIETARY LIPIDS BY CHYLOMICRONS

items.subtopic: TRANSPORT OF HEPATICALLY DERIVED LIPIDS BY VLDL AND LDL
items.assertion: LDL serves a physiologic role in delivering vitamin E to specific tissues.
items.reason: LDL is primarily a by-product of fatty acid energy transport by VLDL with little true physiologic role.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain the assertion since LDL has a specific role in delivering vitamin E.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · TRANSPORT OF HEPATICALLY DERIVED LIPIDS BY VLDL AND LDL

items.assertion: ApoB levels are increasingly considered as a better marker of cardiovascular risk than LDL-C.
items.reason: ApoB provides a more accurate reflection of the number of atherogenic particles in the blood.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why apoB is considered a better marker.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · SCREENING

items.subtopic: DISORDERS ASSOCIATED WITH ELEVATED APOB-CONTAINING LIPOPROTEINS
items.assertion: Intervention in hyperlipidemia can reduce the risk of ASCVD.
items.reason: Hyperlipidemia is associated with increased levels of total cholesterol and LDL-C, which are risk factors for ASCVD.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · DISORDERS ASSOCIATED WITH ELEVATED APOB-CONTAINING LIPOPROTEINS

items.assertion: Familial chylomicronemia syndrome can present with acute pancreatitis.
items.reason: Severe abdominal pain due to acute pancreatitis is a presentation of FCS when fasting TG levels are >500 mg/dL.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · SEVERE HYPERTRIGLYCERIDEMIA (part 1)

items.assertion: Genetic testing is required to diagnose familial chylomicronemia syndrome.
items.reason: The diagnosis of FCS can be made clinically based on HTG severity and history without genetic testing.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because genetic testing is not required for the clinical diagnosis of FCS.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · SEVERE HYPERTRIGLYCERIDEMIA (part 1)

items.assertion: Patients with familial partial lipodystrophy often have severe insulin resistance.
items.reason: FPLD is characterized by increased visceral fat and reduced subcutaneous fat, leading to metabolic complications.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · SEVERE HYPERTRIGLYCERIDEMIA (part 2)

items.assertion: Fish oils or fibrates are effective first-line treatments for familial chylomicronemia syndrome.
items.reason: These agents can reduce fasting triglyceride levels significantly in FCS patients.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; fish oils or fibrates are unlikely to be effective as first-line treatment.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · SEVERE HYPERTRIGLYCERIDEMIA (part 2)

items.assertion: Heterozygous FH is inherited in an autosomal dominant manner.
items.reason: FH is passed from one parent, with ~50% of siblings and children expected to inherit it.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · HYPERCHOLESTEROLEMIA (ELEVATED LDL-C) (part 1)

items.assertion: Homozygous FH results in lower LDL-C levels than heterozygous FH.
items.reason: Individuals with two mutated alleles have much higher LDL-C levels.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; homozygous FH results in higher LDL-C levels.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · HYPERCHOLESTEROLEMIA (ELEVATED LDL-C) (part 1)

items.assertion: Genetic testing should be performed to identify specific causal variants in FH.
items.reason: Identifying specific causal variants has implications for family-based cascade screening.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is a correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · HYPERCHOLESTEROLEMIA (ELEVATED LDL-C) (part 2)

items.assertion: Liver transplantation is sometimes used as a first-line treatment for HoFH.
items.reason: It effectively decreases plasma LDL-C levels in this disorder.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; liver transplantation is considered as a last resort, not first-line.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · HYPERCHOLESTEROLEMIA (ELEVATED LDL-C) (part 2)

items.assertion: Patients with Sitosterolemia often fail to respond adequately to statin therapy.
items.reason: Sitosterolemia is characterized by increased intestinal absorption of sterols and reduced biliary excretion, leading to elevated plasma plant sterol levels.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why statins alone may not be effective.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · HYPERCHOLESTEROLEMIA (ELEVATED LDL-C) (part 3)

items.assertion: LDLR function in cultured fibroblasts is negligible in Autosomal Recessive Hypercholesterolemia.
items.reason: The absence of LDLRAP1 affects the internalization of the lipoprotein-receptor complex.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; LDL receptor function in cultured fibroblasts is normal or only modestly reduced in ARH.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · HYPERCHOLESTEROLEMIA (ELEVATED LDL-C) (part 3)

items.assertion: Patients with elevated LDL-C should be managed with lifestyle modification and medications.
items.reason: To reduce LDL-C levels and the risk of ASCVD.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is a correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · HYPERCHOLESTEROLEMIA (ELEVATED LDL-C) (part 4)

items.assertion: Patients with FDBL typically present in adulthood.
items.reason: FDBL is a recessive disorder characterized by the accumulation of remnant lipoprotein particles.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why patients present in adulthood.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · MIXED HYPERLIPIDEMIA (ELEVATED TG AND LDL-C) (part 1)

items.assertion: The apoE4 allele is associated with FDBL.
items.reason: ApoE4 has an arginine instead of a cysteine at position 112 and is linked to higher ASCVD risk.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the apoE4 allele is not associated with FDBL, but it is linked to higher ASCVD risk.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · MIXED HYPERLIPIDEMIA (ELEVATED TG AND LDL-C) (part 1)

items.assertion: Patients with familial combined hyperlipidemia have a substantially elevated plasma level of apoB.
items.reason: This is due to the presence of small dense LDL particles characteristic of this syndrome.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not directly explain why apoB levels are elevated; it describes a characteristic of FCHL.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · MIXED HYPERLIPIDEMIA (ELEVATED TG AND LDL-C) (part 2)

items.assertion: The molecular etiology of familial combined hyperlipidemia is well understood.
items.reason: No single gene has been identified that causes this disorder in a simple Mendelian fashion.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the molecular etiology remains poorly understood, while the reason is true.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · MIXED HYPERLIPIDEMIA (ELEVATED TG AND LDL-C) (part 2)

items.subtopic: SECONDARY CONTRIBUTORS TO ELEVATED LEVELS OF APOB-CONTAINING LIPOPROTEINS (part 1)
items.assertion: Reduction in carbohydrate consumption can reduce TG levels.
items.reason: Excessive intake of carbohydrates leads to increased hepatic VLDL-TG secretion and elevated TG levels.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · SECONDARY CONTRIBUTORS TO ELEVATED LEVELS OF APOB-CONTAINING LIPOPROTEINS (part 1)

items.subtopic: SECONDARY CONTRIBUTORS TO ELEVATED LEVELS OF APOB-CONTAINING LIPOPROTEINS (part 1)
items.assertion: Insulin resistance leads to increased LPL activity.
items.reason: Insulin resistance results in decreased transcription of LPL, reducing catabolism of chylomicrons and VLDLs.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; insulin resistance actually decreases LPL activity.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · SECONDARY CONTRIBUTORS TO ELEVATED LEVELS OF APOB-CONTAINING LIPOPROTEINS (part 1)

items.subtopic: SECONDARY CONTRIBUTORS TO ELEVATED LEVELS OF APOB-CONTAINING LIPOPROTEINS (part 2)
items.assertion: Cushing's syndrome is associated with increased VLDL synthesis and elevated LDL-C.
items.reason: Endogenous glucocorticoid excess in Cushing's syndrome leads to dyslipidemia characterized by HTG and elevated LDL-C.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · SECONDARY CONTRIBUTORS TO ELEVATED LEVELS OF APOB-CONTAINING LIPOPROTEINS (part 2)

items.subtopic: DISORDERS ASSOCIATED WITH REDUCED APOB-CONTAINING LIPOPROTEINS (part 1)
items.assertion: Abetalipoproteinemia is caused by mutations in the MTTP gene.
items.reason: The MTTP gene encodes MTP, which transfers lipids to nascent chylomicrons and VLDLs.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · DISORDERS ASSOCIATED WITH REDUCED APOB-CONTAINING LIPOPROTEINS (part 1)

items.subtopic: DISORDERS ASSOCIATED WITH REDUCED APOB-CONTAINING LIPOPROTEINS (part 1)
items.assertion: Patients with familial hypobetalipoproteinemia have elevated levels of hepatic fat.
items.reason: This is due to reduced VLDL export from the liver.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · DISORDERS ASSOCIATED WITH REDUCED APOB-CONTAINING LIPOPROTEINS (part 1)

items.subtopic: DISORDERS ASSOCIATED WITH REDUCED HIGH-DENSITY LIPOPROTEINS
items.assertion: Low HDL-C is an important independent predictor of increased cardiovascular risk.
items.reason: HDL-C levels integrate a number of cardiovascular risk factors, explaining its strong inverse association with ASCVD.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why low HDL-C is an independent predictor.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · DISORDERS ASSOCIATED WITH REDUCED HIGH-DENSITY LIPOPROTEINS

items.assertion: Patients with familial LCAT deficiency have very low plasma levels of HDL-C.
items.reason: The enzyme LCAT is responsible for the esterification of cholesterol, which is crucial for HDL maturation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · PRIMARY (GENETIC) CAUSES OF LOW HDL-C (part 1)

items.assertion: Homozygotes for apoA-I deletions have no clinical sequelae.
items.reason: Complete deficiency of apoA-I due to a deletion results in the virtual absence of circulating HDL.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; homozygotes for apoA-I deletions can have cholesterol deposits and premature CHD.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · PRIMARY (GENETIC) CAUSES OF LOW HDL-C (part 1)

items.assertion: Early diagnosis and initiation of therapy for dyslipidemia have proven clinical benefits.
items.reason: Physicians need to screen lipids systematically, rule out secondary causes, suspect inherited disorders, promote family-based screening, assess ASCVD risk, and consider additional risk stratification approaches.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; however, the reason is not the direct explanation for why early diagnosis has clinical benefits.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · Lipoprotein Disorders

items.assertion: Familial chylomicron syndrome should be considered in patients with fasting plasma TG levels >500 mg/dL.
items.reason: Patients with such elevated TG levels are at risk for pancreatitis, which is a characteristic of familial chylomicron syndrome.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · DIAGNOSIS

items.assertion: Familial hypercholesterolemia is always a clinical diagnosis.
items.reason: A finding of a causal mutation may result in earlier and more aggressive therapy to lower LDL-C.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain why familial hypercholesterolemia is always a clinical diagnosis.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Severe Hypertriglyceridemia | LIFESTYLE AND MODIFIABLE FACTORS | PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY
items.assertion: Aerobic exercise can help reduce TG levels in patients with severe hypertriglyceridemia.
items.reason: Regular physical activity has a positive effect on reducing TG levels.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · Severe Hypertriglyceridemia | LIFESTYLE AND MODIFIABLE FACTORS | PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY

items.subtopic: Severe Hypertriglyceridemia | LIFESTYLE AND MODIFIABLE FACTORS | PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY
items.assertion: Fibrates reduce TG levels by approximately 50% in individuals with severe hypertriglyceridemia.
items.reason: Fibrates stimulate lipoprotein lipase activity and enhance TG hydrolysis.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because fibrates reduce TG levels by ~30%, not 50%.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · Severe Hypertriglyceridemia | LIFESTYLE AND MODIFIABLE FACTORS | PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY

items.subtopic: Hypercholesterolemia (Elevated LDL-C with or without Elevated TG) | LIFESTYLE AND MODIFIABLE FACTORS
items.assertion: Intervention to reduce LDL-C substantially reduces the risk of ASCVD.
items.reason: Patients with hypercholesterolemia are at high cardiovascular risk and need intervention.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why intervention is important.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · Hypercholesterolemia (Elevated LDL-C with or without Elevated TG) | LIFESTYLE AND MODIFIABLE FACTORS

items.assertion: The doubling of a statin dose results in approximately a 6% further reduction in plasma LDL-C levels.
items.reason: Statin treatment leads to a dose-dependent reduction in plasma levels of LDL-C.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY (part 1)

items.assertion: Statin therapy should be avoided in patients with renal insufficiency.
items.reason: The risk of statin-associated myopathy is increased by the presence of renal insufficiency.
items.explanation: While the reason is true, the assertion is false; statins can still be used but with caution in patients with renal insufficiency.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY (part 1)

items.assertion: Ezetimibe is generally considered a second-line option for further LDL-C reduction.
items.reason: It is added to statins when additional LDL-C lowering is needed beyond what statins alone can achieve.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why ezetimibe is considered a second-line option.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY (part 2)

items.assertion: PCSK9 inhibitors reduce LDL-C by targeting cholesterol synthesis.
items.reason: They increase the number of LDL receptors available to remove LDL from the blood.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain the mechanism described in the assertion.
items.reference: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY (part 2)
